research.title: CVE-2025-55182 Post-Mortem: What the Next.js RCE Teaches Us About RSC Security
research.whyTrending: A server was compromised 24h after disclosure (front page of HN); attackers targeted crypto credentials with rootkit techniques. Deserializing untrusted client input into module lookups is a lesson worth a deep dive.

gaps: Vendor advisories describe WHAT but not the design lesson for app devs
Nobody connects it to the general "deserialize untrusted input" antipattern history (Java/Ruby/PHP)
No practical detection/response guide for serverless where you cant image disk
Little on what app-level mitigations do and dont help

officialSources.note: Next.js security advisory, affected versions and patches
technical mechanism: Flight deserialization, prototype chain, Function constructor

expertInsights: CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated RCE in react-server-dom-webpack/parcel/turbopack Flight deserialization
Root cause: RSC trusted client-supplied Flight references; no validation on which properties resolved -> __proto__ traversal to Function constructor
A default create-next-app production build was exploitable with zero developer code changes
Disclosed Dec 3 2025 (Meta); exploitation observed from Dec 5 2025
Post-exploit: reverse shells to Cobalt Strike, coin miners, cloud credential harvesting, systemd/cron persistence, dropped binaries
Serverless forensics limited: cant image an ephemeral function
commonProblems: Deserializing untrusted input into code-reachable structures
Trusting a wire protocol not designed as a security boundary
No WAF/egress controls on RSC endpoints
statistics: React ~40% of devs, Next.js ~18-20% of web apps

strategy.angle: A post-mortem that treats React2Shell as the latest entry in a 15-year lineage of insecure-deserialization RCEs (Java, Rails YAML, PHP) — the framework-specific fix is patch-and-move-on, but the durable lesson is that a serialization wire format is not a trust boundary, and RSC quietly made your app deserialize attacker input
strategy.title: React2Shell (CVE-2025-55182): A Post-Mortem on Trusting the Wire
strategy.excerpt: A single unauthenticated POST turned a default Next.js build into a reverse shell. CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell) was a CVSS 10.0 RCE in the React Flight protocol. Here is exactly how it worked, why it is the same bug we have shipped for 15 years, and what it should change in how you think about RSC.
hooks: Fact: a stock create-next-app production build was exploitable with zero code changes by the developer
History: this is Java deserialization, Rails YAML, and PHP unserialize wearing a React hat
Reality: you cannot image the disk of a serverless function that already scaled to zero
outline: Hook: one POST, full shell, no login
The mechanism: Flight, references, and the prototype chain
Why a default app was vulnerable
This is not new: a short history of deserialization RCE
The timeline: disclosure to in-the-wild in 48 hours
Detection and response when there is no disk to image
What actually protects you (and what does not)
The durable lesson for RSC apps
